text: Your abstract was clear, but the conversation after the talk was even better.

I really enjoyed it. I am Patricia, a colleague from the nutrition field. How long have you worked in research?
About ten years. We work in the same field, then -- it is nice to find someone to mingle with who really understands the topic.
Exactly. We have built a good rapport already. Maybe there is room for a collaboration between our teams.
I would love that. Here is my business card. Shall we exchange details?

Welcome to this short session on networking at scientific conferences. For many researchers, the science is the easy part, and the small talk is the hard part. So here are a few simple ideas. First, do not wait for people to come to you. During the coffee break, walk up to a small group and mingle. A safe opening line is a comment about the talk you both just heard. Second, focus on building rapport, not on selling yourself. Ask the other person about their work, listen carefully, and find one thing you have in common. Third, always exchange contact details before the conversation ends. A simple line works well: it was great to meet you, here is my business card, let us keep in touch. Finally, and this is the part most people forget, send a short follow-up message within two days. Mention something specific from your conversation, suggest a possible collaboration, and thank the person for their time. A good follow-up turns a quick chat with a colleague into a real professional contact.

Hi Patricia, this is Mark Davies. We met yesterday at the nutrition science conference, during the coffee break after the keynote. It was really great to talk to you about your work on glycemic control. I am the one who mentioned the cohort study in Lisbon. I just wanted to follow up, as promised. I really enjoyed our conversation, and I think there could be a nice collaboration between our two teams. I am attaching a short summary of our current project, and I would love to hear your thoughts. If you have time in the next couple of weeks, maybe we could set up a quick video call to exchange ideas. Thank you again for the great conversation, and it was a real pleasure to meet you. I hope we can keep in touch. Talk soon.

First, walk up to a small group during the break and mingle. Next, start the conversation with a comment about the talk. After that, ask the other person about their work and build rapport. Then, before you leave, exchange contact details and business cards. Finally, send a short follow-up message within two days.
